"Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day there shall be to you an holy day, a sabbath of rest to the LORD: whosoever doeth work therein shall be put to death." The witnesses note the Sabbath commanded before the work of the sanctuary — the crux of the rest before the work. On "Six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day there shall be to you an holy day, a sabbath of rest to the LORD," the grammatical voices note the Sabbath. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "six days shall work be done, but on the seventh day there shall be to you an holy day, a sabbath of rest to the LORD: whosoever doeth work therein shall be put to death" — the Sabbath was commanded first (before the building of the tabernacle, chs. 35-40), the seventh day a holy rest to the LORD, on pain of death for working; the Sabbath (the weekly rest, the fourth commandment, Exodus 20:8-11; 31:13-17); the death penalty (the strictness of the Sabbath rest).

On the placing of the Sabbath first, interpreters expound the lesson. The reformed and the dominant reading note the order — the Sabbath commanded before the work of the tabernacle; the witnesses note the lesson — the work of God's house, however holy, was not to break the Sabbath (the building of the sanctuary, the most sacred work, must yield to the Sabbath rest); the priority of the Sabbath (the rest of God not to be set aside even for the work of building his house); the holiness of the day (the Sabbath holy, the rest sacred, the figure of the rest in God, Hebrews 4:9-10; the rest before the work, the worship before the labor); the lesson — God to be served in his own way, the Sabbath kept even amid the holiest service.

Wesley and JFB note the death penalty (the strictness of the Sabbath, the sacredness of the rest, Exodus 31:14-15) — the holiness of the day; the providence (the Sabbath guarded, the rest sacred, even the work of the tabernacle yielding to it). The verse displays the Sabbath commanded before the work of the sanctuary.
